meta.title: The Hollow Forest
meta.author: awb_🇨🇭
meta.tone: Gothic horror. The forest is hollowed out — something has hollowed it. Cold prose, slow dread, careful hope. Curses are real and cure is hard. Mercy lives in chapels by day; hunger lives in towers by night.

human.summary: You are warm. You bleed. You age. Most things in this story would prefer you stop being any of those.

revenant.summary: You woke in the graveyard with grave-dirt on your tongue. The cold doesn't bite. Neither do most things.

child_of_the_wolf.summary: Born under a long moon. You knew the forest before the forest was hollowed. The change is already in you.

fallen_knight.summary: You came here three years ago in chainmail and a chapel-sworn duty. The duty is gone; the mail is rusted; the heaviness in your shoulders is yours. You hit hard and tire fast.

village_child.summary: You grew up in the village before it hollowed out. You know which doors lock from the inside, which paths the wolves don't take, and which adults won't meet your eyes. You are small, fast, and hard to surprise.

meta.age_gate_message: The Hollow Forest is a gothic horror story. It contains werewolves, vampires, the death of children, blood-drinking and curses, scenes of villagers consumed by the woods, and themes of grief and slow dread. The prose is restrained but the subject matter is not. Recommended for readers 16 and up.

chapel.name: The Chapel of the Pale Bell
chapel.summary: A small stone chapel, half-roofed, candle-stubs guttering on the altar. A font of clear water stands by the door. The priest is here when the sun is up. The bell-tower stairs climb behind the altar; the path south leads down to the village.
example_descriptions: The chapel is cold but clean. Beeswax and old smoke. Whatever the bell rings for, it has not rung lately.
Light through the broken east window paints the floor. The font's water is clear as truth.

down.message: The trapdoor is bolted from the inside. The priest opens it only for those he means to teach.

bell_tower.name: The Bell Tower
bell_tower.summary: A narrow stone tower above the chapel. A great cracked bronze bell hangs in the rafters. The wind through the louvers carries every voice from the village below. A scratched verse runs around the rim.
example_descriptions: The bell is silent. It has not rung in years; the rope is old and grey.
From the louvers you can see the lake to the south, dark even at noon.

graveyard.name: The Old Graveyard
graveyard.summary: Slate stones in long rows, half of them tilted and unreadable. A fresh trench at the back is empty and waiting. A heavy iron grate at the centre opens to a flight of stairs going down — sealed with a tarnished silver lock.
example_descriptions: Mist clings low to the stones. Something pale moved at the corner of your eye and is not there now.
The newest stones are dated within the last year. The names are family names — all of them.

down.message: The crypt grate is sealed by a tarnished silver lock. You'd need a silver key.

crypt.name: The Sealed Crypt
crypt.summary: A barrel-vaulted stone chamber under the graveyard. The dead lie in alcoves on three sides. Something is breathing here that should not be. A silver-tipped dagger rests on a stone slab — undisturbed for a long time.
example_descriptions: Your torch is small in this dark. The shadows move when you don't watch them.
On the slab the dagger gleams: not iron, not steel — silvered, etched with a verse you can't read.

village_square.name: The Village Square
village_square.summary: A cobbled square ringed by shuttered houses. By day, a few villagers move quickly between doors. By night, every shutter is locked from the inside. A stone fountain runs slow with cold water.
example_descriptions: The square is empty in a way that feels rehearsed. Faces flicker behind shutters and do not come out.
The watchman's lantern carves the dark, somewhere down a side street.

smith.name: The Smithy
smith.summary: A low forge of black brick, banked low when the smith is gone. Iron stock racks line one wall; a rack of fine silver-stained chisels lines the other. The smith works only between dusk and midnight — by day, the doors are barred from inside.
example_descriptions: Coals breathing slow under their ash. The smith's hammer rests on the anvil like a sleeping cat.
Bottles of pale-blue oil stand on a shelf. Silvering oil — you've heard rumours of what it does to the wrong wolf.

forest_path.name: The Hollow Path
forest_path.summary: An old cart-track winds south between black firs. Branches knit overhead so the path is lit only by sky-strips. By day, jays scold from the branches. By night, the jays are gone.
example_descriptions: Bracken and pine-mast. The trees lean inward as if listening.
Tracks in the loam — large pads, longer than a wolf's. Not a wolf's.

west.message: The pack-path bends sideways every time you look at it. As a man you can't go in.

black_lake.name: The Black Lake
black_lake.summary: A still oval of water, dark even by daylight. A stone tower rises from the far shore — one window lit, always lit, never the same window twice. A path leads up to the tower. A submerged stairwell descends below the boathouse.
example_descriptions: The lake does not reflect the sky. It reflects only what stands close enough to see itself in it.
The tower seems closer than it is, then farther. You stop trying to measure.

count_tower.name: The Count's Tower
count_tower.summary: A circular chamber high in the lake-tower. Velvet drapes, a long table, two chairs. The Count is here only between the late hours and the first grey of dawn. By day, the tower is empty — and yet you feel watched anyway.
example_descriptions: The drapes are dust-free. The wine in the decanter is fresh. The chair across the table is — exactly — pulled out.
From the high window the village looks like a coin tossed into the woods.

moonlit_cellar.name: The Moonlit Cellar
moonlit_cellar.summary: Below the boathouse the water gives way to a low stone cellar. Pale light comes from above through cracked floorboards — pale even at noon. The walls are old; the marks on them are older.
example_descriptions: Dripping echoes. A soft scratching from somewhere you can't place.
Bottles in racks. The labels are blank. The corks are sealed with wax stamped with a stranger's sign.

chapel_undercroft.name: The Chapel Undercroft
chapel_undercroft.summary: Stone shelves of bottles, dried herbs in bundles. The brewing-bench is here, where the priest teaches the work to those he believes can drink the cure. A small fire burns in a covered grate.
example_descriptions: The air is dry and cool. Candles in iron sconces. The brew smells of crushed herb and clean water.
A book lies open on the bench — the cure-recipe, copied in the priest's careful hand.

wolf_den.name: The Wolf Den
wolf_den.summary: A hollow at the foot of two leaning firs. The earth here is packed flat by long use. Bones, picked clean. The pack sleeps here when the moon is wrong; the alpha sleeps further in.
example_descriptions: The air is hot, thick with breath. They have been here. They will be again.
A path goes deeper into the brush — west, where no human ever went on two legs.

west.message: The path bends in a way only paws can follow. As a man you cannot find it.

the_hollow.name: The Hollow
the_hollow.summary: A perfect circle of trees, all leaning the same way. The moon is always above this clearing, even at noon. In the centre, a stone, and on the stone, the alpha — first wolf, oldest wolf, the one the forest hollowed itself for. She is waiting.
example_descriptions: No wind. The moon does not move. The alpha breathes slow and old.
The centre stone is warm. Somebody knelt here, once. Somebody made a wolf, once.

east.message: You cannot leave this place as a man. You must finish what you came as.

fen_path.name: The Fen Path
fen_path.summary: An old corduroy road runs east from the wood, sinking into bog. Reeds grow taller than a man. Pale lights flicker among the standing water — never the same place twice.
example_descriptions: Black water on either side. The corduroy logs are slick with moss. A heron lifts and is gone.
Mosquitoes. The smell of cold mud, and underneath it, something the mud has not finished hiding.

mire_grove.name: The Mire Grove
mire_grove.summary: A clearing in the bog. Black willows lean inward, roots half-drowned. A cairn stands in the middle, stones piled by hands that are no longer here. Something below the standing water hums faintly when you stop walking.
example_descriptions: Frogs. Then no frogs. Then frogs again. Whatever quietened them moved on.
The cairn-stones are warm. Someone laid them recently — or they have always been warm.

witch_hut.name: The Hermit's Hut
witch_hut.summary: A low cottage on stilts, wattle-and-daub, roof thick with moss. Bundled herbs swing from every beam. A copper still drips slow at the back. The hermit-witch keeps her own hours, and the door stays propped for travellers.
example_descriptions: Smoke. Wormwood. The hermit-witch sits in a chair by the fire, peeling something pale.
Bottles in racks: clear, smoke-coloured, blood-coloured. A crow lifts from the rafters and resettles.

drowned_shrine.name: The Drowned Shrine
drowned_shrine.summary: A small stone chapel half-sunken in the bog. Only the bell-pediment is dry. The altar is below the water-line; a verse runs around the lintel above the stoop. The candles down there are still lit.
example_descriptions: The water laps the second pew. Candles burn underwater. They shouldn't, and they do.
A drowned figure kneels at the altar. It does not turn when you enter.

sunken_crypt.name: The Sunken Crypt
sunken_crypt.summary: Below the mire, a cold stone vault, water seeping through every joint. Bones in heaps along the walls. Older than the village. Older, possibly, than the priest.
example_descriptions: Cold water around your ankles. Each step echoes wrong, doubled, off.
A stone chest in the centre, half submerged. The lid is tight.

rusty_dagger.description: A workshop dagger — pitted, but it holds an edge.

silver_dagger.description: A long dagger with a silvered tip and a verse etched along the blade.

silver_oil.description: A pale blue oil. Anointing a blade with it makes the blade bite the cursed.

silver_dust.description: Fine, cold to the touch. Useful in oil-making.

silver_key.description: A small key, tarnished black. Cold even in your hand.

holy_water.description: Drawn from the chapel font and blessed. Restores life to the warm; burns the cursed.

wolfsbane.description: A pale herb with violet flowers. Bitter on the tongue. The forest knows it.

moonsilver_herb.description: A grey-leafed herb that grows only on consecrated ground. Soft as cobweb.

garlic.description: A pungent bulb. Folk wisdom claims more than it can prove. Some folk wisdom is right.

torch.description: A pitch-soaked torch. Burns warm, burns short.

bone_relic.description: A small carved finger-bone, tied with red thread. Lucky, the smith claims.

raw_meat.description: Stringy, dark, cooling fast. Better than nothing — barely. Best eaten before it turns.

wood.description: Branch wood. Dry enough to burn, dense enough to whittle.

silver_blessing.description: Distilled wolfsbane and moonsilver in a vial of holy water. Tastes like winter morning. Said to lift the curse from the cursed.

count_signet.description: A heavy ring of black silver, marked with a sign no living priest will name.

wolf_pelt.description: A heavy fur, taken in the dark. The hunter pays for these.

relic_of_origin.description: A small carved tooth on a sinew thong. The first cursed thing the forest ever knew. Cold, even in your hand.

bog_iron.description: Iron rust knit through bog-mat — heavy, dark, faintly cold. The smith pays for clean ore.

hagstone.description: A river-stone with a natural hole through it. Folk wisdom claims you can see truer things through it. Folk wisdom is sometimes right.

witch_water.description: Bitter, herbal, slightly metallic. Restores life. The hermit-witch will not say what's in it.

cursed_locket.description: A silver locket, blackened. Inside, a curl of dark hair and a name etched too small to read in this light.

fen_pelt.description: A heavy, oily skin. The hunter will buy it; better waterproofing than wolf-pelt for boots, she says.

fen_meat.description: Pale, cold-tasting. Eat it warm or not at all.

witch_elixir.description: Distilled from things you'd rather not know. Powerful — and you will feel it before you taste it.

silvered_torch.description: A torch dipped in silver oil. Burns longer; cursed things flinch from its light.

silvered_bolt.description: A short bolt with a silvered head. Single-use; you do not get it back.

bone_amulet.description: A finger-bone strung with silver wire. The smith will tell you it doesn't work. The smith is lying.

lost_sister_journal.description: A small leather book in a girl's hand. The last entry is unfinished.
lost_sister_journal.read_text:
3 December.
Mother sleeps poorly. The watchman walked past three times after midnight; I counted his lantern. Marta says the watchman is a good man. He is, but the village is not. Something is wrong here that nobody will say out loud.

11 December.
Hilde did not come back. The trench is fresh and there is no name on it yet. The priest says she 'wandered'. Hilde never wandered in her life.

19 December.
The Count has invited me to climb. Marta found the note pinned to my door. He writes very neatly. He invites me by name. We are not those kind of people. I should not be on his list.

26 December.
They took Marta tonight. I do not know which of them — the wolves or him. The watchman walked past four times. He counted his rounds; I counted them too. He never went up the path.

27 December.
I will go to the graveyard tonight. I will not let the Count have her too. If I do not come back, do not look for me at the lake.

— Lise

on_read.text: >>> A child's hand, kept careful even as it broke. You close the journal and listen to the wind in the chapel rafters.

gospel_fragment.description: A water-stained page in old script. The verse is worn but the title is clear: 'On Things That Drown And Yet Are Not Drowned.'

brewed_restorative.description: Holy water with crushed wolfsbane and moonsilver. Calming. Strong. The priest doesn't write the recipe down.

woundwort_brew.description: Crushed wolfsbane in warm water. Bitter. Clears a head, closes a small wound.

moonsilver_tonic.description: Pale, faintly luminous. The taste of cold morning. Settles the stomach, steadies the hand.

wolfsbane_oil.description: Bitter, sharp-smelling oil. Coat a blade with it; cursed things flinch from the cut.

count_grimoire.description: A black-bound book on the Count's high shelf. The pages whisper when opened. Reading it does not feel like learning.
count_grimoire.read_text:
I, the unnamed Count, having lived now seven generations of the village below, set down what I have learned of the long dark.

The gift is not given. It is taken — by the giver. The wine in the chalice is yours; the chalice is mine. The night becomes patient with you. The sun becomes unkind. The mortal water burns. You will lose your appetite for what you ate; you will gain another.

My first three centuries I argued with what I had become. The fourth I made peace. The fifth I made a tower. The sixth I made the lake.

If the page becomes warm in your hand, you have already chosen. The change is in you. There is no closing of a door that has been opened by reading.

— Auer

on_read.text: >>> The page warms in your hand. The cold tastes of wine. You will not need to eat the way you did. You have not closed the door.

priest_gospel.description: A small, worn book the priest keeps under the altar. Page after page in his careful hand.
priest_gospel.read_text:
On the cure-of-curses.

The wolf is not your enemy. The wolf is in you, and the wolf is hungry, and the wolf is — yourself, in part. The cure is not killing the wolf. The cure is feeding the man.

Wolfsbane on the tongue, moonsilver on the heart, holy water in the belly. Drink the three together and the wolf turns its face. The man comes back. He does not come back whole; he comes back with the wolf still listening, but listening is not eating.

On the Count.

He is courteous. He is also patient, in the way the lake is patient. Do not climb to him until you are sure of your answer. He will not refuse a guest, but he is not in the business of returning guests.

On the village.

We are forty now. We were a hundred. The trench at the back of the graveyard is dug for two more. I do not know which two. I pray each evening I am not one of them, and each morning I confess the prayer.

— Fr. M.

on_read.text: >>> The priest's hand is steady on every page. Even where it shouldn't be.

hunter_journal.description: A field-journal in a tracker's compact hand. Pelts measured, dates marked, observations cold.
hunter_journal.read_text:
12 February. Three pelts this week, two of average size, one large. The large one had no eyes that worked. The pack does not turn on a blind alpha; the pack does not have an alpha worth turning on.

28 February. The wolf I tracked along the lake-shore was not a wolf when I caught it. It tried to speak. I did not let it.

11 March. The hollow at the heart of the wood — I have heard of it from the priest. I do not believe in such things. I believe in tracks. I will go look for tracks.

22 March. There are tracks. A circle of trees, all leaning. A stone in the centre, scored by something with a paw the size of a serving plate. The alpha is real. She is older than the village. She is — I admit it — older than my charts.

14 April. I will not hunt her. I will sell pelts to those who do, and I will tell them what I have seen, and I will keep my own gun pointed elsewhere.

— K.

on_read.text: >>> The hunter writes the way she walks: quiet, patient, no extra step.

foraging.description: Pick herbs, gather small things from the underbrush.

alchemy.description: Distill, brew, and bottle. Wolfsbane brews close cuts; moonsilver settles nerves. Combine with holy water for the strongest restoratives, or with silver dust for the smith's silvering oil. The cure for the curse — the silver blessing — lives at the top of this skill.

smithing.description: The smith's craft — coax metal, set silver into edge.

lycanthropy.description: The wolf's gift, unasked-for. While you carry it, you can `transform wolf` — fast, fanged, and unwelcome. Dawn ends the change. The priest can lift the curse.

vampirism.description: The Count's gift, asked for. While you carry it, you can `transform bat` — fast, healing on the bite. Sunlight burns whether you're shaped or not. There is no cure.

hunting.description: Track and engage hostile things. The forest has not been short of them lately.

firemaking.description: Coax a flame from kindling. Needed for forge work, brewing, and most cold nights.

hedgewitchery.description: Older than the priest's word, fouler than the Count's gift. Brews that bind the wild instead of taming it. Granted by the hermit's baptism — not taught.

distill_silver_oil.description: Distill silver dust into oil. The smith pays for it; the cursed fear it.

brew_blessing.description: Distill wolfsbane + moonsilver in holy water. The cure for the curse — if the cursed will drink it.

forge_silver_dagger.description: Forge a silvered edge onto a rusty dagger. The smith will do it for you, or you can do it yourself if you've learned the work.

forge_silvered_torch.description: Dip a torch in silver oil. Cursed things flinch from its light.

craft_bone_amulet.description: Bind a finger-bone with silver wire. Folk wisdom, stronger than the smith claims.

forge_silvered_bolts.description: Forge five silvered bolts at a time.

brew_restorative.description: A strong heal for those who drink the priest's brews.

brew_witch_water.description: The hermit-witch's working draught. Strong restorative.

brew_witch_elixir.description: Distilled in iron from things best not catalogued.

brew_woundwort.description: Crush wolfsbane into warm water. Bitter, but it closes a small wound.

brew_moonsilver_tonic.description: Distill moonsilver in holy water. Steady, restorative.

bottle_wolfsbane_oil.description: Press wolfsbane into oil. Coat a blade with it; cursed things flinch from the cut.

werewolf.summary: A wolf, broken-shaped. Larger than it should be. The eyes are not a wolf's eyes. The bite is hot.

revenant.summary: A villager, weeks dead, walking. They do not know you. They do not know themselves.

bat_swarm.summary: A column of dark, moving as one. Hard to see; harder to hit. The cellar is theirs.

shade.summary: A tall, thin shape that bends light wrong. The crypt does not want you here.

grave_dog.summary: A long, lean dog living in the graveyard. It does not bark. It works.

alpha_wolf.summary: Massive, white-eyed, older than the village. The first thing the forest hollowed itself for. It does not run.

fen_wraith.summary: A slow shape of mist and salt-water, knee-deep in the bog. Its hands are empty. Its grief is not.

fen_toad.summary: Heavy, slick, eyes the colour of stagnant water. Bigger than they should be. Bites. Doesn't think.

drowned_priest.summary: A figure in soaked vestments who kneels at an altar that is not there. He stands when you enter. The candles around him have not been lit by living hands.

plague_rat.summary: Larger than it should be. Eyes too bright. The bite carries something the priest doesn't have a name for yet.

sleepwalker.summary: A villager in nightclothes, eyes open but not seeing. They walk where they are pulled. They do not wake when you call.

bell_riddle.prompt: I ring at dawn for those who wake. I ring at dusk for those who don't. I have never rung for the one who waits in the lake. What am I?
bell_riddle.on_solve_message: The verse on the rim catches the morning light, and you read it for the first time. Beneath it, scratched in a different hand: 'Silver waits where silver lay — the grave does not turn the silver away.'

crypt_verse.prompt: I bite the cursed but not the kind. I am cold to the warm and warm to the dead. The grave does not turn me away. What am I?
crypt_verse.on_solve_message: The verse on the slab seems to brighten. Beneath the silver dagger, you find a thin sheet of paper folded in three.

smith_iron.prompt: Born of bog and slept in stone, woken by fire and broken alone, edged by salt and edged by song — I am useful as long as you are strong. What am I?
smith_iron.on_solve_message: The smith laughs, briefly, and tosses you a piece of bog-iron. 'Bring more if you find it. Iron is iron.'

alpha_question.prompt: I had a name once. I have a hunger now. I am older than your village and younger than your forest. What did I lose?
alpha_question.on_solve_message: The alpha looks at you with eyes the colour of bone. The pack moves back. She inclines her head, once.

drowned_verse.prompt: I burn where I am drowned. I sing where I am silenced. I hold where the hand is gone. What am I?
drowned_verse.on_solve_message: A candle below the waterline brightens by itself. Beside the altar, a hagstone rolls free of the silt.

the_priest.summary: A thin, careful man in grey vestments. He is not afraid of you, and you can see he has reason to be.
dialogue_seeds: Sit by the font if you need to. The water is clean.
We do not ring the bell any more. Some sounds find ears we'd rather they didn't.
Whatever you've heard about what stays in the lake at night — believe it.

lines: I see it in your eyes. The change is in you. Don't lie to me — I've seen it before.
There is a way back. Wolfsbane in the forest. Moonsilver in the graveyard. Holy water from this font. Bring them. I'll teach you the work.
You should not be here. Not in the day. Not in this house.
There is no cure for what you have done. Only mercy left, and not from me.
I have heard the village speak of you. A name in the hollow forest is a rare thing.
Sit. You are welcome here as long as you carry no curse.
What brings you here, traveller?

choices.label: I think I have been bitten.

lines: We were a hundred souls a year ago. We are forty now. Most went into the graveyard. A few — went into the woods.
The watchman walks the round still. He carries the silver key for the crypt, when he remembers to.
The Count came thirty years ago. The lake was a pond then. After he came, the pond grew dark, then deep, then — what it is.
Do not go up to the tower at night. Or do, if you've made your peace. He does not lie to those who climb.
Yes. I see. There is a way. Wolfsbane in the forest, moonsilver among the stones, this water in your hand. Bring them. I will teach you the brewing.
If you bring me back the silver blessing — and you have the courage to drink it — the curse breaks.
You have it. Give it to me. Drink. Hold still. ...
It is done. The wolf has left you. Do not look for it again.

effects.text: >>> Your blood quiets. The hunger is gone. You feel only tired.

the_smith.summary: A short, broad woman with arms like cable and a dark patience. She works late, sleeps later.
dialogue_seeds: The forge runs late. The forge runs warm. Bring me work I can finish.
Silver oil's in the rack. Three vials and a rusty knife and I'll silver it for you.

the_watchman.summary: An old man with a brass-handled lantern, walking the round. He nods. He never stops walking.
dialogue_seeds: Stay in by dark, traveller. The lake's bell rings for nobody now.
I carry the crypt's key, when I remember it. Some days I do.

lines: Eh? Oh — yes. The round, yes. Walk with me a bit. The lantern light helps both of us.

choices.label: Tell me about the lost Hilde.

lines: Square at first light, then the smithy at noon, then the graveyard until ten, then the chapel until dawn.
I keep the same paces. The wolves know the paces. They wait for the gaps. The gaps are mine.
Aye. Hilde, of the south house. Walked into the trench last winter. Did not walk back.
I saw her three nights running. The fourth, no. The young one in the chapel — Lise — she took it hard. She took it harder than her mother did. I should have walked her round. I did not.
Don't climb. That's the only thing I tell every traveller. Don't climb.
I have walked past that lake-tower for forty years. I have seen the window light and the window dark and the window where there was no window before. Don't climb.
If a wolf speaks to you, walk away. If it speaks again, run.
If a man at the lake-tower offers you wine, drink water. If he offers you water, drink nothing.

the_count.summary: Tall, pale, courteous. He stands when you enter. He pours for you. The wine is excellent.

lines: So. You climbed. Few do. Most who climb are only curious.
Tell me, then — why are you here?
Curious is honest, at least. Sit. Drink, if you like. The wine will not bite you.
I have been here longer than the village. I will be here longer than the village. The lake came with me; the lake will go with me.

choices.label: What do you want from me?

lines: From you? Almost nothing. A guest is a guest. I want only that one of every hundred who climbs the tower decides, on their own, to stay.
When you decide, you will know. You will come back. Or you won't. Either is acceptable.
You're sure?
It is permanent. The sun will not love you. The mortal water will burn. But you will be patient. You will be many things. You will be — yourself, but more.

choices.label: (But you are already cursed — the wolf would not allow it.)
I'm not sure. Tell me more.

lines: Then take my hand. Hold still.
It is done. Welcome, child. The night is long, and now it is yours.

effects.text: >>> Your blood goes still. The cold tastes of wine. You will not need to eat the way you did.

lines: Ah. Silver. I see. You came prepared.
I will not fight you, traveller. I am very old. If you must, do it. I would only ask you take this — proof, for the priest, that the village is free.

effects.text: >>> The Count slips a heavy ring from his finger and sets it on the table.

lines: ...so it ends. Thank you.

effects.text: >>> The silver bites where silver bites. He goes still. The lake outside ripples once and is calm.

lines: I cannot give what you already have. The wolf is in you, and the wolf is jealous.
Bring me a man, and I might bring back a count. But not before.
They speak of you below, traveller. Even my bats carry the rumour up.
Few who climb to me have a name yet. You have one. Sit.

the_hunter.summary: A weathered tracker who came up from the south the year the forest hollowed. She works the wolf-paths and keeps the village fed in pelts.
dialogue_seeds: Bring me pelts. Three buys you a fair coin.
There's an old wolf at the heart of the wood. Older than the rest. She is what started it.
Walk careful by the lake at night. The tower hears.
Standing rewards: thirty gold for any pelt you bring me, more for the alpha. If you want to make it official — type 'bounty post werewolf 30' — and the village will know.

lines: Sit, then. Pelt rack's full this week — three buys you fair coin if you've brought any.
What do you want to know?

choices.label: How do I track a wolf?

lines: She is real. White-eyed. Older than this village by a long count. The pack does not turn on her; she has no teeth left to bite a wolf with, but they wouldn't anyway.
I saw her, once, from a distance I trust. She is the thing the forest hollowed itself for. End her and the curse-line in this wood breaks. Break it, and the village starts to be a village again.
You don't track a wolf. You track where the wolf has been, and you arrive after. That's most of the work.
Look for breath-fog at chest height in the cold. Look for paw prints that go but don't come back. Trust the silence — wolves move where the birds don't sing.
Silver oil. Torches. A spare striker if you ask. I do not sell silver dagger; the smith does that, when the smith feels like it.
I'll buy any pelt you bring back. Cursed or otherwise. Don't ask what I do with them.
Listen. There is a thing at the bottom of the lake. There is also a thing at the top of the tower. They are not the same thing, despite what the villagers will tell you.
If you climb to the tower, do not look in the wine. If you walk the lake, do not look in the water. That is most of what I have to say about either.

the_hermit_witch.summary: A weathered, grey-haired woman with iron rings on every finger. She nods when you come in. She remembers everyone she has ever poisoned, and most she has not.
dialogue_seeds: Sit, then. The kettle is warm.
Priest's wrong. Count's wrong. Both want you to choose between two cages. There's a third door if you want it.
Bog-iron, hagstone, holy water — bring the three and I'll show you what comes after the last cage.

lines: Welcome, traveller. The fen swallows most who come this far. You're not most.
There is tea, if you have time. There is more than tea, if you have need.

choices.label: Tell me about the third path.
What do you know of the Count?
What do you know of the priest?

lines: The priest will tell you to be cleansed. The Count will tell you to be remade. Both want you tame.
I tell you: there is older water than holy water. The fen baptises its own. It does not cure, and it does not gift. It binds. You become — neither, but kept.
Bog-iron from the path. A hagstone — the shrine knows where they are. A vial of holy water from the chapel font.
Bring me the three. I'll do the rest. You will sleep, then you will rise. The hunger you carried — wolf or other — will not own you.
He is older than this village by a long count. He came up the lake when this fen still drained.
He is courteous. He is also a wolf in his own way. The night-wolves know him; they don't bow. They keep their distance.
He is a good man with a small God. He believes in clean answers. The fen has none of those.
He has lost more than he says. The young woman of the village. She walked into the graveyard and did not come out. He has not slept since.
You have all three. Sit by the fire. Drink. Hold still.
It is done. The fen knows you. You are neither cursed nor cured. You are kept. The hunger that was in you is in the water now.

effects.text: >>> Cold water tastes of iron. The world dims, then sharpens. You are awake in a way you have never been. Your shadow is now part of the fen.

intro_chapel.title: The Hollow Forest
intro_chapel.description: You woke at the chapel. The priest will tell you what little he knows. Talk with him; visit the village; choose your path.
intro_chapel.completion_message: You have your bearings. The priest watches by day, the Count waits by night. The forest is yours to choose.

the_curse.title: The Curse
the_curse.description: You've been bitten. The priest offers a cure: bring wolfsbane (forest), moonsilver (graveyard), holy water (this font), and learn the brewing. Drink the silver blessing and the wolf leaves you.
the_curse.completion_message: The priest takes the blessing from your hand. 'Now drink — and hold still.' (Talk to the priest with `talk priest` to be cleansed; pick the cleansing branch.)

the_gift.title: The Gift
the_gift.description: You have accepted the Count's invitation. He asks one small thing: prove the gift is in you. Feed the hunger three times.
the_gift.completion_message: The Count receives you again. 'Three is enough. The night accepts you.' He pours another glass.

the_slayer.title: The Slayer
the_slayer.description: Some villages do not need a count. Forge a silver-tipped dagger, climb the tower at night, and end this. The smith will help if you bring her silver oil.
the_slayer.completion_message: You bring the priest the signet. He weeps, briefly, and rings the bell himself. The shutters in the village open one by one.

the_silver_key.title: The Silver Key
the_silver_key.description: Climb the bell-tower. Read the verse on the rim. Solve what the bell asks — and the silver lock will yield.
the_silver_key.completion_message: The key is yours. The crypt waits.

pelt_bounty.title: Pelt Bounty
pelt_bounty.description: The hunter pays in coin for wolf pelts — three at a time. Bring her three to start.
pelt_bounty.completion_message: She weighs them, nods once, and pays. "Bring more if you find them."

hollow_origin.title: The Hollow Origin
hollow_origin.description: The hunter speaks of an alpha at the heart of the wood — the first wolf, the one the forest hollowed itself for. Find the relic she wears. Bring it back.
hollow_origin.completion_message: She takes the relic, holds it long enough that you wonder, and gives it back. "It belongs to whoever ends her. That was you. Keep it."

the_lost_sister.title: The Lost Sister
the_lost_sister.description: The priest mentioned a name once: a young woman of the village who walked into the graveyard last winter and did not come back. Find a relic of hers, bring it to him for closure.
the_lost_sister.completion_message: He weeps, briefly, and sets the relic on the altar. "Thank you. The family will come tonight."

the_witch_offer.title: The Witch's Offer
the_witch_offer.description: Visit the hermit-witch in the fen, east of the wood. She speaks of a third path — neither cure nor gift.
the_witch_offer.completion_message: You have heard the third option. Choose.

gather_for_baptism.title: The Fen Baptism
gather_for_baptism.description: The hermit-witch has offered to bind you to the fen. Bring her bog-iron, a hagstone, and a vial of holy water.
gather_for_baptism.completion_message: She has what she needs. Talk to her with `talk witch` to drink the brew. (Pick the 'I have what you asked' branch.)

dawn_revenants.title: Dawn Revenants
dawn_revenants.description: The watchman has seen revenants in the graveyard each morning since the trench was dug. End three of them.
dawn_revenants.completion_message: He nods and shifts his lantern from one hand to the other. 'Tomorrow there will be three more. Come back if you have the stomach.'

the_drowned_priest.title: The Drowned Priest
the_drowned_priest.description: The hermit speaks of a drowned shrine south of the fen-path. The priest who knelt there did not stop kneeling. End his vigil.
the_drowned_priest.completion_message: The candles below the water go out together. The hermit-witch bows her head briefly when you tell her, and pours you something stronger.

plague_in_the_walls.title: Plague in the Walls
plague_in_the_walls.description: Plague rats are nesting beyond the mire. End four of them before whatever's making them spreads.
plague_in_the_walls.completion_message: The rats scatter and don't return for a while. The villagers will sleep less afraid tonight.

the_sisters_journal.title: The Sister's Journal
the_sisters_journal.description: The watchman recalls the young woman writing in a journal up to the night she went. Find it. The priest will want to know what she wrote.
the_sisters_journal.completion_message: The priest reads the last entry, then closes the book gently. He does not look up for some time. 'Thank you. There was something I had not let myself know.'

daily_dawn_revenants.title: Dawn Revenants
daily_dawn_revenants.description: End two revenants before sunset. The trench is fresh again.
daily_dawn_revenants.completion_message: The trench is quieter for a day. The watchman nods once when you pass.

daily_three_pelts.title: Three Pelts
daily_three_pelts.description: The hunter wants three wolf pelts before dusk. She doesn't ask where they come from.
daily_three_pelts.completion_message: She weighs them, nods once, and pays without small talk.

daily_silver_dust.title: Silver Dust Run
daily_silver_dust.description: Bring the smith four pinches of silver dust before the forge cools.
daily_silver_dust.completion_message: The smith sweeps it into a tin and grunts approval. The forge runs hot tonight.

daily_holy_water_run.title: Holy Water Run
daily_holy_water_run.description: Bring the chapel three vials of holy water before the next mass.
daily_holy_water_run.completion_message: The priest accepts them with both hands. 'Bless you. Tomorrow there will be more sick.'

effects.text: >>> The bite burns deeper than a bite should. Something passes from its mouth into yours.
[The sun bites your skin where it touches.]
>>> The villagers do not look up. You are not the strangest thing this year.

blood_moon.title: Blood Moon
blood_moon.description: The moon is full and red over the lake. The forest is hungry tonight. Werewolves rage. The crypt-shades stir. The Count is twice as patient. Survive the three watches.

stages.title: First Watch — the wolves run
stages.narration: >>> The first wolves break cover at the village edge. The watchman's lantern is still.

stages.title: Second Watch — the dead walk
stages.narration: >>> The trench at the back of the graveyard is empty. The earth is loose. They are coming up.

stages.title: Third Watch — the shades
stages.narration: >>> The crypt grate rattles, and what comes through is not a wolf, and not the dead, and not the Count. It is older.

narration.on_start: [A red moon climbs over the lake. The forest exhales. Three watches tonight — wolves, dead, shades. Survive each.]
narration.on_complete: >>> Dawn breaks. The pack is quiet, the trench is filled, the crypt is cold. The village will whisper of you again — and now they will know your name.
narration.on_expire: [The moon pales toward dawn. Whatever passed, passed without you. The watchman shakes his head when you come up.]
